title: Probabilistic Uncertain Reward Model: A Natural Generalization of Bradley-Terry Reward Model
authors: Not specified in the provided text
abstract: This paper proposes the Probabilistic Uncertain Reward Model (PURM), a probabilistic reformulation of reward modeling that intrinsically captures uncertainty from preference data. It generalizes the Bradley-Terry reward model to characterize reward distributions rather than point estimates, and introduces the Bhattacharyya Coefficient to compute uncertainty. The model addresses reward hacking in RLHF by dynamically balancing exploration and exploitation during policy training.

introduction.problem: Conventional reward models based on deterministic Bradley-Terry models suffer from overconfidence when evaluating inconsistently labeled data or out-of-distribution samples, leading to reward hacking where policy optimization maximizes proxy rewards while degrading true performance.
introduction.gap: Current approaches to handling uncertainty lack systematic or theoretical underpinnings, and the standard Bradley-Terry reward model collapses uncertainty into deterministic scalar values.

methodology.key_innovation: Generalizes the Bradley-Terry reward model to characterize reward distributions rather than point estimates
methodology.uncertainty_measure: Bhattacharyya Coefficient to compute overlap between reward distributions
methodology.validation: Simulations of scenarios inducing aleatoric uncertainty (inconsistent labeling) and epistemic uncertainty (OOD samples)
methodology.application: Leverages uncertainty to dynamically balance exploration and exploitation during policy training in PPO by penalizing uncertain rewards

results.effectiveness: PURM generates higher uncertainty values in cases of inconsistent labeling and out-of-distribution samples
results.application: Uncertainty-Aware Reinforcement Learning approach to mitigate reward hacking

conclusion: The Probabilistic Uncertain Reward Model provides a principled approach to uncertainty quantification in reward modeling, addressing the limitations of deterministic reward models and enabling more robust scaling in RLHF for open-ended domains.
